None of the rich, the powerful, the honorable, or the accomplished people in the world are as excellent, useful, or happy as the wise person. Who else can interpret the words of God, or teach correctly from his truths and ways? What madness it must be for weak and dependent people to rebel against Almighty God! Many make wrong judgments, and bring misery on themselves in this life and the next.

God has, in wisdom, kept knowledge of future events away from us, so that we may always be ready for changes. We all must die; no escape or hiding place can save us, nor are there any effective ways to resist. Ninety thousand people die every day, more than sixty every minute, and one every moment. How serious the thought! Oh, that people were wise, that they understood these things, that they would consider their end! The believer alone is prepared to meet this solemn call. Wickedness, by which people often escape human justice, cannot protect them from death.

Solomon observed that many times one person rules over another to his harm, and that prosperity hardens them in their wickedness. Sinners deceive themselves in this. Vengeance comes slowly, but it comes surely. A good person’s days have substance; he lives to a good purpose: a wicked person's days are like a shadow, empty and worthless. Let us pray that we may view eternal things as near, real, and all-important.

Faith alone can establish the heart in this mixed situation, where the righteous often suffer, and the wicked prosper. Solomon commended joy, and a holy sense of security arising from confidence in God, because a person has no better thing under the sun, though a good person has much better things above the sun than to use the blessings of life gratefully according to his position. He would not have us try to explain what God does. But, leaving the Lord to clear up all difficulties in his own time, we may cheerfully enjoy the comforts and bear up under the trials of life; while peace of conscience and joy in the Holy Spirit will abide in us through all outward changes, and when flesh and heart shall fail.
